name: Sardines
summary: Small oily fish eaten whole — so you get omega-3s, vitamin D, and, uniquely, calcium from their soft edible bones, all with very low mercury.
superfoodReason: A low-mercury fish eaten with its bones, combining omega-3s, vitamin D, B12, and a rare animal source of calcium.

benefits.claim: Omega-3 fats for heart and brain
benefits.mechanism: Like other oily fish, sardines are rich in EPA and DHA, which lower triglycerides, dampen inflammation, and are built into brain and cell membranes.

citations.title: Omega-3 Fatty Acids — NIH Office of Dietary Supplements (Health Professional Fact Sheet)

benefits.mechanism: Because sardines are eaten whole with their soft bones, they're one of the few animal foods that supply meaningful calcium — the mineral bones and teeth are built from and that muscles and nerves need to function.

citations.title: Calcium — NIH Office of Dietary Supplements (Health Professional Fact Sheet)

benefits.claim: Also a source of vitamin D and B12
benefits.mechanism: As an oily fish, sardines are one of the few foods that naturally contain vitamin D — which most people otherwise rely on sunlight for — and, like all animal foods, they supply vitamin B12, which vegans and older adults commonly run low on.

citations.title: Vitamin D — NIH Office of Dietary Supplements (Health Professional Fact Sheet)

surprisingFacts.text: Being small and short-lived, sardines sit low on the food chain and accumulate very little mercury — so they're among the safest fish to eat frequently.
